topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers swimming before his eyes. The figures detailing Sarah’s compensation were stark, a painful contrast to his own modest salary. He traced the columns with a fingertip, a tightness constricting his chest. He had worked tirelessly for three years, pouring every spare hour into the projects, yet this disparity felt like a deliberate slight. When Sarah approached him later, her smile bright and breezy, he managed a tight nod. "That's just how the structure works, Elias," she chirped, completely oblivious to the tremor in his hand as he pushed the papers back across the desk. He felt a deep, internal resistance rising, a refusal to accept the reality presented. He resolved, silently, to simply move on, to focus on the next task, letting the uncomfortable truth settle like dust on an old filing cabinet.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" Mark asked, his voice laced with disbelief as he looked at the pay stub. He leaned back in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning under his weight. A hot flush crept up his neck. He had spent the last six months meticulously documenting his contributions, the late nights, the extra initiatives he’d spearheaded. Now, seeing the figures, a strange hardening settled over his features. He picked up his pen, his movements precise and deliberate, refusing to let his gaze linger on the discrepancy. When his supervisor called him in for a discussion, Mark met his eyes squarely. "I understand the numbers, sir," he stated, his tone level, almost cool. "But I'm confident in the value I bring to the team." He felt a stubborn set in his jaw, a quiet declaration that he wouldn't let this single data point derail his confidence.

[story 3]

I reread the email three times, the words blurring into a frustrating haze. It was undeniable; the financial gap between me and Ben was significant. A knot tightened low in my stomach, a heavy, unwelcome sensation. I walked to the window, watching the city lights blur into streaks of neon. I wanted to confront him, to demand an explanation, but the words caught in my throat, lodging themselves like grit. Instead, I spent the rest of the afternoon cleaning my workstation with an almost obsessive fervor. Every wipe, every polish, was an attempt to exert some control over the swirling uncertainty. I felt a resolute stillness settle over me, a quiet decision to process this internally, to maintain a certain composure despite the unwelcome knowledge.
